title: OCI LiveLab
text:
SSH Keys
cd .ssh
ssh-keygen -b 2048 -t rsa -f <<sshkeyname>>


IAM

Created a compartment named “demo”
Crated a domain within demo called “LiveLab”
Created a group within that domain called “oci-group”
create user “TestUser” to be part of “oci-group”



VCN

Created a VCN through wizard called “OCI_HOL_VCN”


IP RANGE: 10.0.0.0/16, Public 10.0.1.0/24, Private 10.0.2.0/24


Created a Security group that allows SSH and TCP Traffic



COMPUTE

Created a Compute Instance
Updated Yum and installed HTTP
Configured Apache

sudo yum install httpd -y
sudo apachectl start
sudo systemctl enable httpd
sudo apachectl configtest
sudo firewall-cmd --permanent --zone=public --add-service=http
sudo firewall-cmd --reload
sudo bash -c 'echo This is my Web-Server running on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure >> /var/www/html/index.html'

SG already allowed TCP



Block Volume

block volume is created
attached to instance
Run iSCSI commands
mount drive

ls -l /dev/oracleoci/oraclevd*
sudo mkfs -t ext4 /dev/oracleoci/oraclevdb
sudo mkdir /mnt/disk1
sudo mount /dev/oracleoci/oraclevdb /mnt/disk1
df -h


Object Storage

created object storage
uploaded file
link: link

title: OCI Puppet LAMP Stack
text:
The environment consists of 1 Server Node which is a (VM.Standard.E2.2) Shape runnning ubuntu, and the another VM called ClientNode1 Which is a VM.Standard.E2.Micro running ubuntu
My LAN is 10.10.1.0/24
Please ensure that port 22 and 8140 are allowed on each machine’s firewall, and that both machines can reach one another (i.e. ping or traceroute)

title: OCI Puppet LAMP Stack
text:
Setting up hostnames

For the Client and Server to recognized one another, hostnames must be given on both machines.


Note: I use Helix, but nano, emacs, and vim is fine

sudo hx /etc/host   
[insert ip] puppetserver
[insert ip] puppetclient

title: OCI Puppet LAMP Stack
text:
Installing Puppet
It’s safe to assume, apt has been updated and upgraded.

Puppet Server
The puppet server is used as the main controller for each puppet agent/client. For instance, I am running a lamp stack config on my server, and so when I edit the config; I can push those edits remotely to each puppet agent on a machine. Further, I can also request those edits from the agent itself.
And the puppet server is only to be installed onto the server node/machine.

Running Apt

wget https://apt.puppetlabs.com/puppet8-release-jammy.deb
sudo dpkg -i puppet8-release-jammy.deb
sudo apt update
sudo apt install puppetserver

Editing Puppet Memory (if needed)

The -Xms1g and =Xmx1g is where you can edit, 1g simply means 1 GB of memory
sudo hx /etc/default/puppetserver
JAVA_ARGS="-Xms1g -Xmx1g -Djruby.logger.class=com.puppetlabs.jruby_utils.jruby.Slf4jLogger"

Service management

sudo systemctl start puppetserver
sudo systemctl enable puppetserver
sudo systemctl status puppetserver


Puppet Agent
Likewise, assume apt is updated and upgraded.
The Puppet Agent is just the client, so only client machines/nodes need it.

Installing via Apt

sudo apt install puppet-agent -y    

Editing Config

sudo hx /etc/puppet/puppet.conf 

Add the following to the config

[main]
certname = puppetclient
server = puppetserver   
If you gave different names to your hostnames, then make sure to follow that.

Service Management

sudo systemctl start puppet
sudo systemctl enable puppet
sudo systemctl status puppet

title: OCI Puppet LAMP Stack
text:
Certifcate Management
On the Puppet Server Node, do the following:

List Pending and Available Certificates

sudo /usr/bin/puppetserver ca list --all    

Sign those certs

sudo /usr/bin/puppetserver ca sign --all

title: OCI Wazuh Deployment
text:
What is Wauzh?
As stated before, Wazuh is a SIEM solution, and is comprised of four main components:

Wazuh Indexer stores data and alerts which the Wazuh Server creates.
Wazuh Server serves as the analytics engine which detect threats or anomalies that are collected from endpoints.
Wazuh Dashboard visualizes alert and data from the Wazuh Server and Indexer, and highlights where agents are deployed.
Wazuh Agent(s) deploys on endpoints to forward collected data to the Wazuh Server.

Further, all four components must be running together, otherwise Wazuh will not function correctly.
For further visualization on how these four components work together, refer to the following diagram from Wazuh.



Wazuh Architecture


In essence, the agents forwards data from endpoints to the server which generates alerts for the indexer to store, and all of this information can be viewed from the dashboard.

title: OCI Wazuh Deployment
text:
Deploy with OKE

Configuring OKE Cluster
OKE allows for two creation methods for clusters which are Quick Create or Custom Create. For this blog, we will be using Quick Create as it provisions the Virtual Cloud Network (VCN), Internet Gateway (IG), NAT Gateway (NAT), Service Gateway (SGW), Cluster, and Node Pool for us.
When creating the OKE cluster through Quick Creation, we will expect similar values for the following:



Component
New value




Cluster
AB-Quick-Cluster


Cluster type
Basic


Kubernetes
v1.36


Network type
OCI VCN-native


Kubernetes API Endpoint
Private


Node Type
Managed


Kuberenetes Worker Nodes
Private


VCN
Quick Create-generated VCN


VCN CIDR
10.0.0.0/16


Private API subnet
10.0.0.0/28


Private API endpoint
10.0.0.11:6443


Worker-node subnet
10.0.10.0/24


Service LB subnet
10.0.20.0/24


Kubernetes Service CIDR
10.96.0.0/16


Network Security Groups
None


Security Lists
Three Quick Create-generated lists


Node Count
3


Node Shape
VM.Standard.E5.Flex


Number of OCPUs
2


Memory (GB)
8


Node Image
Oracle Linux 8



Do note that our networking configuration may differ, so do take note of your given values. Additionally, you may add another subnet that allows for public addressing, and a separate security list for access.
For this blog, the following subnet and security list has been created in the OKE VCN for Wazuh Access via a Compute Instance through a local forwarding SSH tunnel. This instance will be our jumpbox.



Component
New Value




Compute Subnet
10.0.30.0/24


Security List
OKE-Wazuh-Compute


Security List Ingress
SSH from 0.0.0.0/0


Security List Egress
All to 0.0.0.0/0



These are just example values, so please ensure the values match your environment.


Configuring Wazuh for the Cluster
There are multiple methods on accessing the private OKE cluster to configure Wazuh which includes OCI Bastion Service, OCI Shell, or via a Compute Instance. We will be configuring Wazuh via a Compute Instance is on the same VCN as the OKE cluster and can access its nodes.
For now, I have created a simple Oracle Linux 8 Instance that is on the 10.0.30.0/24 subnet to serve as our jumpbox. Once, the instance is the instance is running SSH into it.
ssh -i <ssh-key> opc@<public address of the instance>
Afterwards, install the OCI CLI.
Next, we will be configuring the kubeconfig for our private endpoint. You may need to configure the OCI-CLI config file, and the guide can be found here.
mkdir -p "$HOME/.kube"

oci ce cluster create-kubeconfig \
  --cluster-id <YOUR-OKE-CLUSTER_OCID> \
  --file "$HOME/.kube/config" \
  --region us-ashburn-1 \
  --token-version 2.0.0 \
  --kube-endpoint PRIVATE_ENDPOINT
Additionally, we will need to install Kubectl onto our instance which can be found at the oracle cloud native environment guide and the install kubectl guide.
Then, we can clone the Wazuh-Kubernetes repository onto the jumpbox.
git clone https://github.com/wazuh/wazuh-kubernetes.git \
  -b v4.14.6 --depth=1

cd wazuh-kubernetes
Next, generate the self-signed certificates for Wazuh.

./wazuh/certs/indexer_cluster/generate_certs.sh
./wazuh/certs/dashboard_http/generate_certs.sh
Once the certificates been generated, the values within the Kubernetes manifest must be updated to leverage OCI internal load balancers

Updating the docker images in the deployment files
The following files located in the wazuh-kubernetes repo need to have the image value updated.
wazuh/indexer_stack/wazuh-dashboard/dashboard-deploy.yaml
wazuh/indexer_stack/wazuh-indexer/cluster/indexer-sts.yaml
wazuh/wazuh_managers/wazuh-master-sts.yaml
wazuh/wazuh_managers/wazuh-workers-sts.yaml
Within each yaml the image value will be formated as the following wazuh/<service>, please update it to docker.io/wazuh/<service>. This is done to avoid CRI-O name errors.
For example, in wazuh/indexer_stack/wazuh-dashboard/dashboard-deploy.yaml
The image value is wazuh/wazuh-dashboard:4.14.6, so simply update the value to be docker.io/wazuh/wazuh-dsashboard:4.14.6. And do this to each of the previously mentioned files.


Updating Service Manifests in Wazuh to use Private OCI Load Balancers.
The default manifest values are not tailored for OCI, so we must update them. And the files for which need to be updated are the following
wazuh/indexer_stack/wazuh-dashboard/dashboard-svc.yaml
wazuh/indexer_stack/wazuh-indexer/cluster/indexer-api-svc.yaml
wazuh/wazuh_managers/wazuh-master-svc.yaml
wazuh/wazuh_managers/wazuh-workers-svc.yaml  
For dashboard-svc.yaml, we will commend out non-OCI statements and add service.beta.kubernetes.io/oci-load-balancer-internal: "true" within the annotations section.
The yaml file will appear similar to the following:
# Copyright (C) 2019, Wazuh Inc.
#
# This program is a free software; you can redistribute it
# and/or modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public
# License (version 2) as published by the FSF - Free Software
# Foundation.

# Dashboard service

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: dashboard
  namespace: wazuh
  labels:
    app: wazuh-dashboard
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/oci-load-balancer-internal: "true"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: wazuh-dashboard
  ports:
    - name: dashboard
      port: 443
      targetPort: 5601  
For wazuh-master-svc.yaml and wazuh-workers-svc.yaml, the annotations section to be the same as the example above.
Lastly, indexer-api-svc.yaml will have the type variable updated to type: ClusterIP as shown in the following example.
# Copyright (C) 2019, Wazuh Inc.
#
# This program is a free software; you can redistribute it
# and/or modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public
# License (version 2) as published by the FSF - Free Software
# Foundation.

# Indexer service: API

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: indexer
  namespace: wazuh
  labels:
    app: wazuh-indexer
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: wazuh-indexer
  ports:
    - name: indexer-rest
      port: 9200
      targetPort: 9200



Validate and Deploy Wazuh
Render before applying to validate the manifest.
kubectl kustomize envs/local-env/ > /tmp/wazuh-rendered.yaml
Then deploy.
kubectl apply -k envs/local-env/
To check pods status, use:
kubectl get pods -n wazuh -w
Once the pods are in the running state use the following to confrim connectivty between Wazuh-Dasboard to Wazuh-Indexer, and Waazuh-Dashboard to Wazuh-Manager. A 401 Unauthorized Error is expected, and highlight that authentication is enforced.
kubectl exec -n wazuh deployment/wazuh-dashboard -- \
  sh -c 'curl -skI https://indexer:9200/'

kubectl exec -n wazuh deployment/wazuh-dashboard -- \
  sh -c 'curl -skI https://wazuh:55000/'
To get the private Dashboard load-balancer address:
kubectl get svc dashboard -n wazuh
SSH into the jumpbox, with the following parameters to establish a forward local port on 8443 to connect to the private Dashboard load balancer.
ssh -i <ssh-key> -N -T -o ExitOnForwardFailure=yes \
  -L 127.0.0.1:8443:<dashboard-private-ip>:443 \
  opc@<jumpbox-public-ip>
And now on your workstation’s browser, open https://localhost:8443 Since the certificate is self-signed, there will be a warning. However, proceeding pass the warning will open the Wazuh Dashboard
To get the password of Wazuh, use the following within the jumpbox:
kubectl get secret indexer-cred -n wazuh \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.username}' | base64 --decode; echo

kubectl get secret indexer-cred -n wazuh \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 --decode; echo
